Here is a well-formatted, punchy review for Apex Legends tailored for Steam. I’ve broken it down into "The Good" and "The Bad" so it’s easy for other players to read, and included a TL;DR at the bottom. The Best Movement Shooter Around (With a Catch) After hundreds of hours in the Arena, Apex Legends remains one of the most exhilarating yet deeply frustrating battle royales on the market. If you are looking for a fast-paced shooter with a high skill ceiling, this is it—but go in with your eyes open. The Good Unmatched Movement & Gunplay: The gunplay is crisp, and the movement mechanics (sliding, wall-bouncing, zip-lining) feel incredibly fluid. No other battle royale matches this pace. The Legend System: Every character feels distinct. The synergy between different Legends' abilities allows for incredible tactical depth and clutch plays. Ping System: Even years after launch, the ping system remains the gold standard for communication without needing a microphone. High Skill Ceiling: Winning a fight feels genuinely rewarding because it requires a mix of mechanical aim, movement mastery, and positioning. The Bad The Solo Queue Experience: Playing this game without friends can be brutal. The matchmaking frequently pits casual players against top-tier "Apex Predators," leading to a lot of frustrating wipes. Server Stability & Audio: Audio issues (like silent footsteps) and occasional server lag have been persistent thorns in the community's side for seasons. Aggressive Monetization: While it’s strictly cosmetic, the aggressive push for expensive collection events and skins can feel exhausting.

I thought I was starting a game about killing monsters. Instead, I found a story about family, love, friendship, loss, and the people we would cross the world for. Geralt's journey to find Ciri is one of the most memorable stories I've experienced in gaming. But what truly made this adventure special were the people around him. Yennefer and Triss aren't just romance options on a menu; they're characters who feel real. They have flaws, dreams, fears, and histories that make every interaction meaningful. There were moments that made me smile, moments that broke my heart, and moments that made me sit quietly and think about the choices I had made. Few games manage to make you care this much. The world is beautiful, but its greatest strength is its soul. From the muddy roads of Velen to the icy winds of Skellige, every place feels alive. Every quest feels like someone's story rather than another task to complete. When the credits rolled, I wasn't thinking about the monsters I had slain or the loot I had collected. I was thinking about Ciri. I was thinking about Yennefer. I was thinking about Triss. I was thinking about the friends I met and the goodbyes I never wanted to say. The Witcher 3 didn't feel like a game I finished. It felt like a world I had to leave behind. ❤️ "In a world full of monsters, the only thing worth fighting for is the people we love."

Just when I thought I was out, they pull me back in! This is the kind of journey that inspires worlds, art, music, other games, other worlds. The amount of work that went into each aspect is something remarkable, the world is beautiful, characters well crafted, the story lines are bound to leave a mark, the mechanics standard setting for the time period. When you take all the pieces together, I don't know of another game that has accomplished all that this game has, at least to the level of quality on display here. Truly, this was the "next gen" and may just live forever on my top 10 list. But like many of the greatest games ever made, somehow what stuck with me the most to this day is the music and sound design team. They truly went above and beyond to create something not just memorable, but actually evocative, haunting and uplifting at times even- right up there with the other greats of the genre like Oblivion and Dark Souls (although it could be argued in terms of both fictional and irl anthropological scope that the Witcher 3 might actually surpass both of those games regarding the depth of effort put into its music and sound design, if such a thing could be truly quantified...). I loved the Witcher 1 & 2, and I think that the more linear direction of the second game might have actually been superior for servicing the story compared to the fully open world of this game. However, the trade off allows for some seriously cool moments, plenty of added player freedom, traversal on foot, horse, and boat, and it makes the world feel much more alive. Plus we get some really cool systems featured like the weather patterns, and of course, Geralt's beard physics. This title was also one of the last games I can recall pre-purchasing without regret (read: yes there were some terrible and hilarious bugs on launch and the game def improved over time but it was a barrel of fun even back then watching Roach twerk through collision with a boat). After my last ng+ deathmarch play through I was pretty ready to be done forever with the game having completed everything save for the odd rare achievement. But now CD Projekt Red has announced new DLC will come out next year... (I wonder if other companies will realize there are other games, older, great games on functional engines, just waiting for a dev to get the go ahead to add new quests.) "Great, another portal..." [steam was offline for half of my play time, save file is approx. double the 360 hours listed here at time of review]

I played this game upon release in 2015 on console. It became my all-time #1 game. Over a decade later, in the big 2026, I've returned to this game on PC. It's still my all-time #1 game. It's as close to a perfect game as you can get. Worldbuilding, combat, characters, quests, and graphics - all outstanding. I'd highly recommend playing this game on the Death March difficulty, as it gives you a truer Witcher experience in the game. Relying on potions, oils, bombs and researching the monsters you hunt gives the game much more depth. Take your time, soak in the game. Complete treasure hunts, side quests and Witcher contracts along the way. Don't blitz the game. I'd happily lose my memory to experience this game again for the first time.
Don't skip the side quests, explore everything in the map and you'll have a meticulously handcrafted gaming experience

The story is pretty good. The combat is fine. The leveling system is bad. The card mini-game is surprisingly good. The main story and side quests are pretty okay. You don't get a lot of decisions. The side quests mostly boil down to "go to place, click on clues, kill monster". That's fine, but it gets a little thin after the first dozen hours. If the combat was more satisfying, it would be great. I'd go click on clues for some sekiro-tier boss fights. But most of the combat is very generous and not very deep. You have huge iframes on dodge, and no stamina management for most of your options. It stops being a fun challenge and quickly becomes a chore. Aggravating that is how the leveling is bad. Early on, simple enemies like a pack of wolves can be a serious threat. Later, you out-level most things and even being chomped on repeatedly by wolves won't make a dent. Some monsters are arbitrarily high level, which turns you into a frustrating one-hitpoint-wonder if you try to do them too early. But do them too late, and the terrifying monster of the woods collapses like a paper tiger. Equipment also has levels, which even further aggravates the problem. Putting aside the absurdity of "you are too low level to wear this shirt", it adds a sort of gear grind that just isn't fun or needed. You already out-level stuff. You don't also need to double your sword damage. It would be a much better game without the leveling, where monsters threat mapped to what they are in the world instead of a floating number over their health bar. It's jarring that the protagonist, who has been doing this monster hunting business for years, will have his power dramatically spike in the few in-game weeks the game covers. At least the card game is surprisingly good. It's still worth playing, but it's imperfect. Runs okay on the steam deck, too.

Review – The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt Complete Edition (Steam) After many years, The Witcher 3 still feels like one of the best RPGs ever made. Few games manage to combine storytelling, world-building, memorable characters, and meaningful side quests at this level. 👍 What I Loved Outstanding story and characters. Geralt, Ciri, Yennefer, Triss, and many others are written incredibly well. Side quests often feel better than main stories in other RPGs. Choices have real consequences, sometimes hours later. The world is beautiful and full of interesting stories to discover. Hearts of Stone and Blood and Wine are among the best expansions ever released. 👎 What Could Be Better Combat is serviceable but not the game's strongest feature. Movement can feel a bit awkward at times. Some points of interest, especially in Skellige, can become repetitive. The first few hours may feel overwhelming due to the number of systems and quests. ⚖️ Final Verdict The Witcher 3 is the complete package: an incredible story, memorable characters, meaningful choices, and hundreds of hours of content. Even years after release, it remains the benchmark for story-driven open-world RPGs. Recommended 👍 10/10 – A masterpiece that every RPG fan should experience at least once. 🐺⚔️ "Evil is evil. Lesser, greater, middling... makes little difference."

10/10 Deserving of all the hype and acclaim. The best Western RPG ever made. Just get it now before the DLC and play on Death March difficulty for a extremely immersive Witcher-sim, were every bomb, potion, and oil can be the difference between life or death. The star of the show is the world and atmosphere. It is so relaxing and immersive to just ride around, listening to all the dialogue and admiring the still stunning graphics and god-tier soundtrack. The game really makes you feel like a badass monster hunter, drifting though towns and cities looking for work. The story is great, with the voice acting and dialogue in particularly being industry best. Everyone, even random NPC villagers in ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ nowhere, give great performances. The world is brutal and unforgiving, with many plots and quests having no real "everybody wins" option. People act logically and in their own interests and Geralt can choose how involved he becomes in the dilemmas of others. The side quests and contracts are also fantastic, with many RPG's know mimicking some of the elements from this game. The DLC's will be their own reviews, but both are great. With Blood and Wine being even better than the main story and featuring my personal favorite area/setting in any game. The combat is more fun on the harder difficulties, but it does feel a little dated and can get repetitive. The abilities and loadout mechanics are also a little restrictive once you get to higher levels. But this is remedied by the DLCs, which introduce new abilities for Geralt. There are a lot of segments where you investigate bodies or locations for clues, and all of them are well-done and logical for a man who has superhuman senses and reflexes. The game doesn't really do a good job of explaining the crafting system, but craft the Witcher gear and keep the monster parts from the beasts you slay. Get this game. It's a masterpiece and one that might not be surpassed anytime soon. I've done 4 full runs + DLCs and I'm still fiending for more.

180 Hours. Thats how long it took to finish the game, and both expansions. I went out of my way to do every single witcher contract. Every Sidequest. Everything. This game made me OBSESSED about the world I was playing in. And the simple act of just... being a Witcher was also shockingly fun. Because 7/10, you're being hired to fight and kill monsters, whether or not you kill them, how you find and fight them... all of that is left in your control. The game does not feel like it is wasting your time. The pacing is amazing, the optional things are truly optional. The voice acting is superb, as is the facial expressions and mocap work. The villains are believable, the flaws in heroic characters are as well. You can play the horny witcher and bang every sorceress you find (Just dont try that with Triss and Yen specifically, it will horribly backfire) And blood and wine, blood and wine is the MOST FUN ive had in an expansion. One that makes the "happily ever after" ending feel EARNED. and now just given. And on top of all of that, the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ MODDING COMMUNITY man! You want to get back into this and shake things up, you can find mods for just about every ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ part of the game. Including adding the systems from previous titles in the series. This, is not a fast game. This is not a game youre going to finish in a week. Give this series the time, and it will take you on a magical ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ journey. Good luck on the Path.

This is not just my favorite RPG. This is probably my favorite game, period. The world is ridiculous in the best way. You ride into some muddy village thinking you’ll clear one tiny question mark, and three hours later you’re dealing with a cursed family drama, a monster with better lore than most main villains, a morally impossible choice, and somehow a quick round of Gwent because apparently Geralt has time for cards while the world is falling apart. That’s what makes it special. The side quests don’t feel like filler. They feel like short stories with real characters, ugly choices, and endings that don’t always let you feel clean. The world is beautiful, sad, funny, cruel, and alive. Velen, Novigrad, Skellige, Toussaint — every place has its own mood, music, problems, and people you remember. Geralt is also one of the best main characters in games. Tired, dry, dangerous, secretly soft, and constantly dragged into everyone else’s terrible life decisions. The writing, music, atmosphere, monsters, DLCs, and little quiet moments all come together into something that still feels huge and personal at the same time. I love this game. Not “yeah, it’s good” love. More like “I was supposed to do one quest and now it’s 3 AM and I’m emotionally attached to a horse, a bard, and a deck of cards” love. Absolute masterpiece.

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt Complete Edition is one of those rare games that completely pulls you into its world. What impressed me most is the depth of the storytelling. Even side quests often feel as meaningful and well-written as the main story, with choices that have real consequences and characters that stay memorable long after you've finished their quests. The world feels alive and immersive, from the war-torn villages of Velen to the bustling streets of Novigrad and the breathtaking landscapes of Skellige. Every location has its own atmosphere, history, and stories waiting to be discovered. The Complete Edition adds two incredible expansions that could easily pass as full games on their own. Combined with the outstanding writing, rich lore, and immersive world-building, this is one of the best RPG experiences I've ever played. Highly recommended for anyone who enjoys deep stories, meaningful choices, and getting lost in a beautifully crafted fantasy world.

The Witcher 3 is a game that will stay on the "must play" list for many years to come, for people looking for a story-focused experience. Side quests make sense in-world, and the emotional weight of NPCs do a lot of heavy lifting in them; many actually will influence and expand on the main story. It's not Fallout 4 when it comes to the interpersonal relationship between Geralt and his adopted daughter, Ciri; there's an emotional connection there that makes you want to see what happens, whether or not you find her. This game emphasizes why story isn't an afterthought in a game, and why game developers should spend time and resources into its crafting. Then, you have the DLCs, which continues the trend, and expands the world in some eerie and dark, or fairytale and whimsical ways, with what is one of the best written villains in all games ever.

7/10. Not a game that I would recommend in 2026 but nonetheless I enjoyed it. Playing this game in 2026 feels like a game that you are playing a less polished game which a bunch of QoL issues. However given this game is made in 2015, that is understandable. I would give credit to the game on its character writing (strongest point), music and voice acting. Everything else in the game feels outdated. I do like the idea of having 3 main endings however I would appreciate it more if the developer does give some hints as to which of the dialogue options that would ultimately affect the endings as I felt there are so many dialogue options given but only like 5 of them actually matters to the final ending. The character progression system in the game is a bad game design in my opinion. This is because you are capped by 12 skill slot when there is 80 skills to choose from. Further more, these 80 skills is split into 4 section and within each section is a skill tree. This means you need to invest certain skill points into the skill tree before unlocking better skills. However there is no telling of how good the skills is as there is no skill preview of how the skill works and how effective it is. There is also no reset skill tree option if you want to try other type of skills. Basically you are stucked with the same skills throughout the game. I did not touch on the gear crafting section of the game as it once again is another mess. Some gear requires certain higher level NPC to craft them and the game does not tell you where to locate this NPC. There is also no tracking in the game regarding the crafting item required which makes crafting equipment a headache. I am also not the biggest fan of the side quest in the game which revolves around the events of the main quest. The side quest is missable if you advance to far into the main story and certain side quest will be affected by the choices you made during the main quest. However, I do give credits to the side quest as it does keep me intrigued and it is not boring and repetitive like the side games in the other games. The combat of the game felt okay and nothing too innovative in my opinion. You basically just smash the same two buttons of dodge, hit, dodge, hit when it comes to facing the bosses as the magic spells takes too long to cast.

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt remains one of the best open-world RPGs. The true magic of the game lies in its incredible sense of immersion and deep, authentic lore. Every village feels lived-in, and even the smallest side quests are written with the care of a main storyline. If you are an experienced gamer, I highly recommended playing on the hardest difficulty, especially if you plan on using a guide or looking up builds. Because coming prepared with the right oils, potions, and signs completely changes the dynamic of a fight, preparation can make the game feel significantly easier. Upping the difficulty preserves the tension and rewards that preparation. I enjoyed reading and learning about the monsters while preparing. It built up tension before a big fight. However, if you are a completionist aiming for that 100% run, be prepared for a bit of a slog. Tracking down every single Gwent card across the continent can turn from a fun tactical minigame into a genuinely tedious chore. I would like to note that I have the platinum trophy on playstation and will eventually get it on PC as well - so it's not THAT bad! If you play it to get lost in an unforgettable world and enjoy a strategic combat challenge, it’s easily a 10/10.

I had originally played the game when it first released on GOG. I immediately recognized Witcher 3's greatness when I began sinking my teeth into it. After devouring the main game and the 2 incredible expansions, I reluctantly put the game down and began playing other stuff. I relatively recently decided to repurchase the game on Steam to more easily play the game on the Steam Deck. I wanted to experience the game again, but didn't know how it would hold up so many years later. I am simply amazed. I'm not sure whether it's more of a testament to how far ahead of their time the developers were or how most major game developers these days are dropping the ball, but the Witcher 3 still stands out as an incredible achievement. What helps cement this sentiment is the outstanding work CD put into updating the game for the modern era. It's a next-gen update in every sense of the term. I am blown away how amazingly well the game runs and looks in 1440p. I began my most recent playthrough of the game with a GTX 1080 with high settings and it ran silky smooth. I then upgraded to a RTX 4060 which only made the game look that much more stunning. I am very happy that I can continue to recommend the Witcher 3 all this time later without hesitation.

Elden Ring is one of the rare games that genuinely feels like a masterpiece. The world design is absolutely unmatched. Exploration doesn’t feel like filler—it feels like discovery in the truest sense. Every direction you go has something worth finding, and the game constantly rewards curiosity without holding your hand. Combat is deep, satisfying, and incredibly flexible. Whether you’re playing a heavy strength build, magic, or something more hybrid, the game gives you real freedom to approach situations your own way. Boss fights are often highlights of the entire experience, with many feeling unforgettable once you finally overcome them. What really makes Elden Ring stand out is how consistently it delivers those “I can’t believe this is in a game” moments. The scale, atmosphere, and sense of mystery are on another level. There are some rough edges—occasional performance issues and some repeated enemy designs—but they don’t take away from the overall experience. This is easily one of the best games ever made. Not just an action RPG, but a landmark title that sets a new standard for open-world design. Highly recommended.

HOW DO I NOT HAVE A REVIEW FOR THIS GAME??? I got this game YEARS AGO, back in college, as a gift from a good friend of mine, Jay. He got it for me as an introduction to souls-like games, and I had the blessing of having him join me for my first boss fight. For fans of souls-like games: This is a great one, and you already know you're in for a good time with a Fromsoft title. Get the game, dog; and don't give up, skeleton. Souls-like games are best captured, in my opinion, by this game's opening sequence. You leave the tutorial area, and right in front of you is a massive horseback sentinel. It deals enough damage to two-tap you. Your hitbox is incredibly precise, and its hitbox is incredibly precise. With enough skill, you can do some damage to it, and survive for a while. The game places it there to tell you "Some obstacles are hard enough you should go find other loot, level up, and return stronger to better deal with them; however, you will be rewarded for your skills and will be able to accomplish anything you set your mind to with enough skill, time, and willpower." With that lesson placed in front of me, I spent 9 hours straight, running on like 22 hours of being awake when I first started, beating this boss, and me and my friend celebrated its glorious defeat. And that is how I got introduced to souls-like games. The game went on to delight, frustrate, and otherwise completely enthrall me. It's seeded a deep love of soulslike games, and has provided already 400 hours of thoroughly enjoyed gameplay, with countless more yet to come as I continue to play through the 100% playthrough I'm working on right now, of which I'm at 53% completion with 177 hours. Not including the DLC. This game rocks.

Elden Ring is genuinely one of the best games I’ve ever played. It’s not perfect, some bosses are annoying and the questlines are basically impossible without looking stuff up, but the game is still insane. The map feels massive without being empty, every random cave or elevator can lead to something cool, and there’s always some weird weapon, boss, armor set, or hidden area to find. The combat is hard but addicting. You can get destroyed for an hour, change your build a little, learn the fight, then suddenly win and feel like you actually earned it. That’s what makes the game so good. It doesn’t just hand you wins, it makes you lock in. The build variety is probably my favorite part. You can run strength, bleed, magic, faith, dex, whatever cursed setup you want, and somehow it can still work if you build it right. Honestly, I get why people call this game a masterpiece. It’s frustrating sometimes, but in the best way. One of those games where you think you’re only playing for 30 minutes and then somehow it’s 4 AM. 10/10. I hate this game sometimes, but I love it way more.

The premise of Elden Ring was really quite simple. You know this iconic series of challenging action games FromSoftware is known to make? Take that and make it bigger, grander and epic. Make it Dark Souls, but open world. Then give GRR Martin yet another excuse not to finish the Winds of Winter by hiring him to create for you a backstory full of weird incestuous genderfluid gods. Finally, add to the mix a strange horse-goat of a mount and you have something really quite unusual. That's the premise and the promise. Now whether it delivers on that promise or not that's a different discussion. One that after spending a good 200 hours on this game's expansive world, perhaps I can answer. Let's begin with the elephant in the room. The open world. This is one damn big elephant, truly ginormous. It goes on and on. Mountains, swamps, frozen over lakes, caverns, vast underground locations. This is one of the bigger games I have played and it's filled with details. It's also terrifyingly beautiful. The rich use of colour, the stunning landscapes, the dramatic vistas, the incredible heights. The game's art direction is top-notch. I would argue it's era defining. However there is an important caveat to consider. The open world is an incredible achievement yes, but how well does it serve the game's other mechanics. Here the picture becomes somewhat less celebratory. You see the game retains its Dark Souls bones, the nuanced, environmental story-telling, the sparse quests driven by occasional meetups. Only now this spans a vastly larger surface and becomes a lot harder to experience naturally. Put simply, a great number of quests are next to impossible to do correctly without a guide. Not because of the minimalist design and lack of compasses, but exactly because of how incongruous the quest design is when applied to an open world. You execute a step in a quest. The character moves. Where? Go figure. In Dark Souls progression was linear and this was not much of an issue. Here, it is. On gameplay this is largely Dark Souls still. Except significantly easier. This is likely another side-effect of the open world direction. I'm not saying the game is not challenging. Rush ahead and you will be punished. However, the game rewards careful exploration, as it rewards skilful combat. Generously. And there is plenty of rewarding side-content to conquer. Do that enough and by the endgame even superbosses won't be that scary. On a smaller scale, a disappointing element was the complete lack of a swimming ability. So many inviting islands close to the coast that are impossible to reach without gimmicks. The moment your character reaches the darker shade of waters down they go, as if they reached the proverbial end of the world. Be that as it may, this is a competently made and entertaining action game. Given its pedigree, I would expect no less. Technically speaking the game is less impressive than its stellar art direction might suggest. Its graphical prowess in particular is decent but not extraordinary. There are sloppy technical decisions present that I wouldn't expect to see in a modern game. For example the lack of ultra wide monitor support. Even Dark Souls Remastered had that. But not Elden Ring? What's worse, the game is taking anti-cheating a bit too seriously and attempting to mod the game to include missing functionality can mark you as a cheater. I ended up having to play the game completely offline and with the use of extraneous software that didn't integrate quite seamlessly enough. All so that I could make the game work properly with my setup. FromSoftware really needs to do better here. To conclude, does Elden Ring deliver on its promise. Is this Dark Souls but open world and is that a good thing? The first question is easy. Yes, it definitely is. The minimalist story telling, the challenging combat, the well crafted world. It's all here. The second question is harder. Dark Souls is not an open world game and making it one required changes that occasionally felt like discounts to the formula. In the end this is a lovingly made game of incredible ambition. It is beautiful and it is flawed. Is it worth playing? Absolutely, it's also worth pondering. There is something truly monumental just a few improvements away.

Once you look past the fact that this game is stuck at 60 FPS, this is genuinely the greatest of all time. I'm usually a books are more enjoyable than videogames kinda guy, but Elden Ring's world building is just unbelievable. Some of the bosses make you want to rip your hair out, but every experience feels cherished and dear in hindsight (except for some of the despicable dungeon puzzles that have no reason existing). The game has been on my backlog for a while, and I'm glad I finally got around to playing it. Side note for PC players with steam decks: Once you get to basically end game, it's really easy to experience all three of the main endings (the ones that give achievements) without being forced to create a new save or new game+ for other endings. Make sure to have a save on your steam deck prior to choosing an ending, and set your PC to offline mode. If you move your character even an inch to the right on your steam deck with cloud save, your PC local file is now desynced from your steam deck. This causes a file conflict which you want. You can go through each of the ending, and go back to your cloud save from the steam deck so you can choose another ending.

YOU PROBABLY SHOULDNT BUY IT. all these reviews are really basic and lack any depth, everyone on steam is saying the same worn out phrases(vastly open world!, new bosses everywhere!, beautiful scenery! best game ever 1000/10") you'll find on ign and gamespot, thus alot of these people can't seem to form their own opinion or even, the same opinions in different words, so i went crazy with this review. I'll start by saying, graphics are subpar for its time, not too big a deal, but this isn't the game you would boast on a 4k monitor. graphics are good but in comparison with other games they aren't the BEST, yes there are some scenic spots where the game is beautiful, but as for 2026? ., its a tad underwhelming compared to new games and even other games older than it. MY FIRST 'SOULS' GAME Got this game couple weeks ago, was looking for a story mode game, had an itch to be a samurai/swordsman that NEEDED to be scratched, thus the purchase of this game, IF you're wanting to play this game the way I described myself playing it, YOU WILL (likely) NOT HAVE A GOOD TIME. (at least if you aren't prepared) playing games like Black Myth Wukong, Skyrim, Read dead2, I wasn't sure what to expect the game is hard, but its less a skill issue and more of a time spent/leveling up issue. you will NOT be able to play the storyline quests outright, you actually HAVE to go explore, and level up for HOURS before even becoming remotely capable to continue the storyline. for ex, if you googled where to go after beating the first storyline boss, 80% of sources will say you should not progress, and instead level up running around at any direction basically. you spawn into a MASSIVE world full of enemies, secret entries, tunnels, etc. that has almost no npcs whatsoever, the WHOLE GAME has less than 100 npcs entirely which is crazy to me, being skyrim has over 10x the npcs at 1/3 the map size. anyone thinking this is "the best open world game ever" clearly haven't played that many OpenWorld games. also You aren't pointed in any direction , its not a clear cut path on what to do pretty much at any point, in fact after the tutorial, you cant even beat the enemy non-bosses upfront of you (unless you picked the right starting class, AND you're a skilled gamer but still unlikely .) last but not least, people hype this game up, you know the types... "its so hard!'' "you think you can beat it? i did it was easy" ''you just gotta..'' then reality: you beat this game by running around and leveling up. 75% skill tree/magic/enchantments 25% raw reflexes and skill. the biggest challenge you'll be faced with is time spent playing the game. you would need to drain ATLEAST 110+ hours into this game just to beat the "40 hour" storyline. how would a 40 hour storyline take 1-2 hundred hours? you have to level up the entire time, doing literally whatever you want, which has its perks. the storyline which notably lacks in cutscene and dialogue. at least to me personally, there isn't a lot of personification with the protagonist(player) or even dramatic/cinematic buildup that you would expect considering a fiction writer somehow took place with development.? do i like it? sure. would i recommend it? only if you have patience, and a LOT of time. is it a good game? in alot of ways no, and yes. what's the point of customization if you have to use the same broken builds to beat ending bosses? what's the point of a free world when there's no npcs, buildings, houses, villages etci? do you like starting a game to not be able to truly access the story line for 10+hours? do you like constantly googling where you need to go or what to do next in a game?

Its fine. I wish there was a neutral option, cause I think while this game is good, its a big step back for FromSoft. There are a lot of problems with the game which are only exacerbated by the DLC. Boss design is the worst it's ever been, and I don't think there's a single truly great boss in the game. The open world is great at first, but becomes repetitive quickly as the game goes on, as there's not much new content and unique bosses that get introduced in the later regions. The magic that these games once had is fading away as FromSoft reuse cheap tricks to make bosses artificially difficult and immersion breaking. The game as a whole lacks innovation and creativity, which it should have in spades considering FromSoft have been using the same formula for their games for decades. Imo, they played it too safe with this game. Elden Ring tries to appeal to everyone but consequentially lacks the uniqueness that makes these games special.

I was excited to try this game because of the 10/10 reviews and George RR Martin's involvement. But sadly the world just feels so...empty and forlorn. I get that its supposed to be post apocalyptic dark fantasy, but you'd think there would be at least be some kind of narrative and more than a handful of NPCs that only babble nonsense. The game is absolutely mind-blowing in its visuals. Even in the spell animations. They're some of the best I've ever seen, in fact. The world is gorgeous and the art direction team deserves some heavy praise. But this illusion of immersion shatters the moment you realize there isn't much to do while exploring that beautiful world aside from randomly killing things to grind XP. It actually baffled me quite a bit. I'm not the biggest fan of Game of Thrones, but Martin obviously knows how to write fantasy lore. But I didn't see any of that in my nearly 10 hours of play. I was lost and confused and wondering what my motivation was, what I was supposed to do and why I should care about this world. So I did some research, and it turns out that he didn't write any dialogue or narrative for the actual game, but just the lore that happened 5,000 years before the game started, which the player has no way of accessing. I don't really see the point in this. If they were going to hire someone to create an elaborate fantasy world, why didn't we get to play in that world, instead wandering around randomly fighting stuff in an empty world of nothing? Perhaps the gameplay could have supported the experience, but I found it quite lacking in that department as well. I elected to play a mage, and the only useful spell I could find was the generic magic missile starting spell. There were others, but the selection was very limited, I had to grind runes to get them, and even then I could only access them after fighting a secret boss that could only find by looking it up on a wiki. (Seriously, there was no clue about this in the actual game itself.) After going to all this trouble, what did I get? A handful of useless garbage spells that were worse than what I started with. So yeah, my impression of this game is probably tainted by the poor implementation of magic. But I don't think I would have enjoyed playing a martial-type character either. The melee combat is slow and sluggish. It doesn't feel quick and responsive at all, more like moving through a layer of mud, and evasion is awkward. Character progression is likewise pretty lackluster. All you get on level up is 1 point of stat assignment that barely changes your numbers. No feats, skill trees, talent points, anything like that. It didn't feel like I was making a build or learning new abilities that changed how I played, more like just grinding to make numbers go up. All of this only got worse when I ran into the first major boss. I wasn't strong enough to beat him despite being almost level 30. I'm not sure how much more I would have had to grind to get past it, but I was already bored by that point. The kicker, though, was when the game randomly deleted all my save files. I might have been halfway tempted to keep going, but not if I have to start over now on top of all of that. I honestly don't understand the positive reviews for this game. Even 15 year old games full of jank like Oblivion and Morrowind have better worlds, more complex magic systems, and are more creative than this.

Elden Ring was my first Soulsbourne game/Fromsoft game back on the PS5 and I have 1500 plus hours on it. I loved the game so much that I bought it again on PC once I had a good gaming rig. Because of this game, I recently went back and played all the Dark Souls games and enjoyed the hell out of them as well, so that's another reason I decided to pick this one up again. After playing those, I have some different thoughts and insights on this game. Either way, I suppose it might be helpful to break this down into a Pros/Cons list, so here we go: Pros: -Visually, this is the most beautiful game I've ever played. I wish it had a legit photo mode because I've taken so many screenshots, it's not funny. Unfortunately, most of those have health bars or something in them. The views often look like a painting and are truly a work of art. And, no, it's just the skyboxes, it's the architecture, overall building design, and locales as well. -Best exploration of any open world game I've played - there aren't any icons or checklists, you just explore, get some visual storytelling, and you are frequently rewarded with cool items, lore, and/or visuals. I played FF VII Rebirth after this and as fun as most of the game is, the open world was... mostly unpleasant. I wish they could have learned from this game. -Weapons, armor, and items - oh my. This really creates a big part of the replayability of this game as you can come up with so many new "builds" and new ways to play. -Replayability - Continued from above, but yeah... it's hugely replayable between getting different endings, finishing up questlines you missed, and just being able to play through it completely differently. The latter was my favorite part as I played through the first time with a Dex/Bleed build and had several struggles toward the end, but when I played as sorcerer I breezed through some of those areas and struggled in different places. -Challenging, but rewarding (most of the time) - At first, the combat was painful for me. I had never played these games before and it just wasn't clicking. It took way longer than it should have and I tried to play it like a Skyrim stealth archer, so you can imagine how that went. Still, once it began to click, it became more fun. When you do overcome an obstacle or a tough boss, you feel good about it. -Spirit Ash Summons - I'm so glad these exist. I'm currently trying to play without them, unless it's like a reused boss or something just to add some variety, but these helped so much on my first few runs since this was all new to me. I'm not sure if I would have finished the game without having them as an option. Of course, it's pretty clear after playing the other 3 games that the bosses here were designed with their use in mind. Cons: -Most NPC questlines are borderline impossible without guides - This goes for all of the Souls games, but this one the most. I completed some of them by accident, but for most of them, I didn't even realize they were quests. I don't think we need a detailed quest system or anything, but a basic journal would be great. I played Shadows of the Erdtree with some hand-written notes and it helped, but this game is just too big to remember all these NPCs, what they want, and where they're going. Well, you never know where they're going... -Endgame difficult spike - Mountaintops of the Giants and Consecrated Snowfields are just unpleasantly difficult. Part of it is the enemies and bosses just have huge health bars, but also they hit ridiculously hard even at high levels. In addition, some of the catacombs are almost a joke with their difficulty such as when they include some of the difficult early catacomb bosses as regular enemies throughout. I found myself getting frustrated a lot and in subsequent playthroughs, I skipped a lot in these areas unless I'm looking for specific items. -Reused bosses - I noticed this more after playing the DS games and coming back to ER, but it is just not fun running into your 4th, 5th, or 10th Erdtree Avatar, Ulcerated Tree Spirit, or Crucible Knight. I hate it even worse when they just become a regular enemy. It's not quite as bad if they don't respawn, but it's still just boring. Shadow of the Erdtree did this the worst with the Ulcerated Tree Spirits, especially in one of the coolest areas of the game. It's like the only thing that changes is that they're stronger and hit harder. -Shadows of the Erdtree empty areas - This expansion is beautiful, challenging, and a game in and of itself, but there are some big, empty areas. I get that some are important for the lore, but I'm not sure why they couldn't have cut some of these down and/or added some other items worth finding. The Finger ruins, the grassy areas outside of Shadow Keep, and the area with the red flowers are some of the worst offenders. Again, at least they're all really cool looking, but I got an empty feeling quite often after exploring them. I found myself asking, "Is that it?" quite often. -Shadows of the Erdtree final bosses and overall ending to the DLC: Just overall I would say the final bosses and overall ending were... disappointing. I don't want to give spoilers, but Gaius and the final DLC boss are just awful. Endless combos, bad hitboxes, and flashiness to the point of distraction. It was just so unfun that I had no problem looking up "cheese" methods or even doing a respec just to get through the fights. Anyway, overall this is one of my favorite games of all time. It's easily top 5 or maybe even top 2. I really hope there's an Elden Ring 2 that takes what this game did and even further improves upon it. I'll be there for it, either way.

Do you hate playing video games, but love watching movies and doing other things in general? Well, then this is the game for you! Have fun as it plays itself, any time you do anything wrong, because you haven't gotten the frame perfect timing down for hundreds of moves that are literally impossible to intuitively react to with clunky ass basic game mechanics, that make you wonder if they even wanted to make a good game with smooth, interactive gameplay again after Dark Souls III and Sekiro. Of cause they kept the ♥♥♥♥♥♥ stamina mechanic from Dark Souls and the nonsensical scaling of your levels and the rest of the game in new game cycles, which just make playing the game unrewarding as ♥♥♥♥, though. But quality of life improvements like rolling out of a landing animation or being able to cancel an animation into another move? Nah, that sounds like it could be enjoyable. Relish in your other hobbies while the game cheeses you to death, then just does nothing for a while, then tells you that you died (in case you didn't notice), then just unnecessarily reloads, instead of just letting you immediately reset the fight like any of the mods there are. And then depending on area you're gonna have a lovely run back across vast landscapes where every single rock (and they are everywhere) is a time sink. Lastly find motivation to become a programmer, as this game has mind boggling programming issues (just like the earlier titles) like several seconds of lag any time you open a menu or simple text message. Or just ignores your inputs. Or stores them, but in a way that kills you. But hey, maybe you're just interested in the lore, the world building, the story. And there is so much to discover! Well, nothing is actually conclusive and you're gonna have to spend hundreds of hours fishing for information and looking up the ♥♥♥♥♥♥ quests online, but wannabe Tolkien helped write it, so maybe just make yourself believe it's good.

This was my first Fromsoft game. I was scared to play it because of the notorious difficulty their games often have, my boyfriend encouraging me to try it and friends followed. Reluctantly I gave in and now this is by far one of the best games I've ever played. Story, vibes, music, gameplay and most of all general feel. I find it to be a fantastic video game just across the board and recommend it to truly everyone. It's only hard if you refuse to learn from your mistakes and can't look within for your error-- It's generally very fair and forgiving when you take your time and take heart to every small visual lesson provided to you. There's a playstyle for a little of everything. Even if you're an aggressive player like me, there's builds you can depend on and thrive. The magic feels good, the Oonga Boonga strength builds feel insane and best of all the huge assortment of incredibly designed outfits you'll find across the complexly built world. Every twist and turn rewards the greediness of adventuring-- Or punishes you delightfully for trying. I love Elden Ring and I don't think I've left the story the same person as when I started.

This is the coziest of cozy games, and one of my favorite games. I don't typically enjoy games like this, but ever since I've played it, and again, and again, I've tried looking for other games like it. I think the closest one would be Graveyard Keeper, but it still doesn't hold a candle. I think I've done 3 farms at this point, and it seems like each farm is ~150 hours. Legitimately do not know how I've accumulated that much time. I mean, I do, because the hours just melt away when you're playing this, but it's astonishing at how fast that happens. I've never tried mods for this, but I have heard the modding community is quite massive, and there are a ton of amazing mods out there... maybe my next farm I'll look into it. But I'm pretty sure the game has a native way of creating/adding mods, and whenever a developer does that, you know they care about their product. The only faults I'd find in this game would be feature requests. I almost want this to be more like Zelda style combat, castles, and more equipment, but then you'd come back to your farm, take care of your animals, brew potions, maybe temper your equipment, whatever... then back out to the castles, or just stay on the farm doing your thing. You could extend that to full randomization, and maybe there was a certain win condition or something, but at that point, this would be an entirely different game. Now that I've gotten that out of the way, I am really looking forward to the Haunted Chocolatier because that might be my feature requests above. And if not, the creator clearly puts in his best effort and creates quality products, so I'm sure it'll be even cozier.

I have some problems with Stardew Valley in general, but that isn't for my to get into a bunch of rambles about. Ultimately it is a fun life/farm sim game where you get to enjoy growing your crops and animals. The townsfolk are entertaining enough, but I do wish more of the romanceable characters were interesting. A lot of the character arcs are less arcs and more so things happening, which may be par for the course for these kinds of games. However I still would have liked more. In spite of this, I find the gameplay quite fun and the music is absolutely gorgeous. My personal favorite track is "Nocturne of Ice." Actually, I'll go on one ramble. I'll never get to say this anywhere else ~ A lot of players do not like the winter season. I have heard a lot of these people say that winter is the most boring part of the game. However I think we should be real here. Winter is Not for growing crops, it is special time for you to explore the entire rest of the game. Winter, especially the first winter, is a really good opportunity to get further in the mines, upgrade all your tools one after the other, and upgrade buildings while also focusing on your animals instead of your crops. Winter is also a great time to do fishing, and there are quite a few birthdays to give gifts for. This all to say, Winter is a fun season. Maybe it isn't the bread and butter of the game but it is the delicious homemade jam on the side. And I do quite like the jam. I expect most of the people reading this review are probably people who have already played the game. However, if you're interested in Stardew Valley, I suggest trying it out on whatever platform you prefer. I also suggest checking out a Story of Seasons game, as well as Fields of Mistria. Stardew might turn out to be your favorite, but these games are good as well, and sometimes you want something Like Stardew, but not quite like it.

I have nearly 300 hours now on this game on the PC alone, not including the Mobile and Switch versions. I adore this game, definitely got a little addicted for a time. I don't just enjoy Stardew Valley; I love everything about it. The pixel art aesthetics are timeless, bringing warmth to every season and NPC interaction. But beyond the beautiful graphics, it's the cast of characters that truly pulls you in - each one feels genuine, and their stories become your own. Performance-wise, this game is rock solid. In hundreds of hours, I've hardly experienced a single crash, which is rare these days. Even when I started adding mods to expand the creative horizons, it remained stable (my first crash only happened after I went very deep into the mod list!). Finally, there's the community. Anyone I meet who shares this love for Stardew Valley is charmingly pleasant to talk to - it says something special about the kind of players this game attracts. Whether you're playing vanilla or using creative mods to reshape the farm, this game offers endless joy. If you're looking for a game that respects your time and your heart, look no further. 10/10.

As someone who had bought this game years ago and didn't touch it much as I just couldn't bring myself to find much interest in a farming simulator, I have to say that was one of my biggest mistakes I have made in regards to judging a game by its cover. This game is not just a farming simulator like it is commonly described, it has so much more within it through the storytelling of the characters, the cute aspect of raising animals and finding spirits in the community center to help restore it to its past glory, exploring mines and caves in not only the main town but also in other locations through the bus, and a lot more I have yet to discover apparently. I genuinely cannot recommend this game enough to someone who just wants a calming game to play since it encourages all sorts of playstyles. From casual to challenging this game has been a blast. And to anyone who has played this game a lot and found themselves burnt out of the normal content I highly recommend looking into the modding community with this game as there are entirely fanmade cities and campaigns of this game that expand the game from its already long runtime into something completely timeless. I would give names of mods but I myself haven't been able to get into that aspect just yet as I have been wanting to continue my vanilla journey before venturing into that realm; but I highly recommend looking into it from the people I've talked to about this game.

Team got too big for its britches over time and quality was VERY noticeably left by the wayside, and I really don't see things improving, at least as of June 10 2026. Every update, the game seems to want to follow its own rules less and less, and break more and more, and the devs are making some serious headscratchers for decisions, like... who wanted the doors to have momentum and block and move items, exactly? Why did we need to learn AGAIN how to test for Yurei, just for that stupid feature that adds nothing of value? These devs take a very enthusiastic "if it isn't broken, break it" approach, as evidenced by that and various other issues (both by design and not) that came with the most recent major update. So yeah, I've certainly had my fun, it was absolutely a great game before, but I definitely can't call it one in this increasingly broken state, no way, and I dread to see what 1.0 is going to look or play like. Steering clear of these devs in the future, for sure.

4 years since I was gifted this and somehow I never reviewed it?? well, better late than never I suppose! Phasmophobia is an intriguing multiplayer supernatural-horror/thriller experience, you play as paranormal investigative contractors- in each contract you play, you have one goal, use your various equipment items (more are unlockable as you progress, and different tiers too!) to identify and confirm the type of supernatural entity residing within whatever haunted location, there's maps such as a prison ,a school, and various different houses. You *can* play it singleplayer, but in my humble opinion, it's far better with friends, even just one other person, though it's good fun for a group! hop on a discord call, or use in-game proximity chat to talk & work together- the gameplay is solid, and all around an engaging, and somewhat addictive experience, and this game is genuinely a labor love, all these years gone by and it's still actively being worked on, it's so much more polished than it was in 2022! Highly recommend, even for non-horror fans!~

Played for years and it was a nice chill horror game to play with friends The latest update rolls around, and with it came animations with every single interaction, all of which slows down the game dramatically. The quick responsiveness of interactions with things like smudge sticks are a thing of the past with this new update. Now you have to wait a few seconds for everything, and in the heat of the moment against a ghost, those few seconds might as well be a few years. They also added character customization, but only in name. The items that the give you in the way of shirts, hairstyles, and so many other options that you would expect in a character creator are surprisingly limited. Think a max of 3 or 4 outfits regardless of how far you've made it into the game. I won't even get into the character models. This game still has a lot of the charm that made me fall in love with it back in 2021, but this update removed a large portion of that charm, and I honestly cannot recommend anyone spend their money on the game in its current state

This was our go-to game. We played it pretty much daily. We had a lot of fun with it. Genuinely enjoyed the game, the weekly challenges, events- etc. However, after the new update it's been.... so unpleasant. :( We basically stopped playing because it's just not fun for us anymore. We keep trying to come back to it, but end up switching to an entirely different game to play. There are way too many glitches, and unnecessary features that I'm not sure anyone really wanted or asked for. I understand that the Phasmo team spent a lot of time, manpower, and effort on this...that they had high hopes for the update, and were excited to show everyone. They had great intentions, and wanted everyone to love it after all the effort put into it. That being said, sometimes I think the best thing a game's team can do is acknowledge when they messed up. Kill switch the new changes, and revert back to what it was before the update. Go back to the drawing board to figure out where things went wrong, and fix it. Get play testers for it that can give quality feedback before putting it out for everyone. I don't think it's a wise choice to double down on an update that made the game worse simply because there was a lot of time and manpower invested into making it. Sunk cost fallacy. I do, however, send my thanks to the Phasmo team regardless of the current unpleasant state of the game. I wholeheartedly had so much fun playing this game with my friends for years, and we have a lot of great memories and goofy moments because of it. I hope to eventually come back and play.

I am so incredibly frustrated with this game that I had to add onto the pile of complaints already on here. The first time I played Phasmophobia was about 6–9 months ago. My wife found the game, we pitched it to another couple we’re close with, and the four of us instantly loved it. Around that same time, I had recently gotten my wife a gaming laptop, but she preferred playing on Xbox, so I used the laptop. A few games in, I realized the game had VR support. I had a Meta Quest 3 sitting around that I’d barely touched, plus an expensive link cable, so I figured why not try it. I played Phasmophobia in VR one single time and genuinely never went back to flatscreen after that. I loved it. That game completely got me into VR gaming. I downloaded Half-Life: Alyx, the Lethal Company VR mod, the Half-Life 2 VR mod, everything. Phasmophobia was basically the gateway into VR for me. And now this update has me unbelievably disappointed. I’m someone who’s usually very easy to entertain when it comes to games. I loved Call of Duty: Ghosts when it came out. I loved Assassin’s Creed III when it came out. I’ve never really been the kind of person screaming “this update ruined the game” every time something changes. But this time? I honestly get it. Before this update, yeah, there were occasional bugs, but nothing major. I could play for 4–5 hours straight with friends and maybe only have to restart my headset once or twice. Now VR feels borderline broken compared to what it was just a few weeks ago. And the actual VR gameplay itself feels awful now. It genuinely feels like a cheap knockoff version of the game I was playing before the update. Everything feels worse. I almost need to hear from other VR players just to make sure I’m not going crazy here. I even saw someone say VR might actually be better after the update, and I genuinely do not understand how that’s possible unless I’m somehow doing something wrong.

I've spent a few hundred hours with Phasmophobia over the years since its release, and at this point, I just can't recommend it. Old bugs have never fixed. Lighting issues have persisted, and somehow have gotten worse. The new character customizations and animations are uncanny at best, and downright non-functional at worst. Its sad to see a game that had such unique mechanics, a satisfying gameplay loop, and a dedicated community being squandered so much. All the recent updates have been a step in the wrong direction, and have turned what used to be a janky (but charmingly so), fun experience into what looks and feels like a broken, rushed mess. Given its former popularity, one would expect Kinetic Games to invest in some professional levels of art, animation, and back end code, but instead we get crossovers no one asked for and jittery animations that hurt to look at. I likely won't be playing it again.

Phasmophobia is what happens when a group of friends decides that walking into a haunted house at 2 a.m. with a flashlight from the dollar store is a fantastic life choice. At first, everyone acts like a professional paranormal investigator: "Let's gather evidence and stay calm." Five minutes later: "WHY IS THE TOILET WHISPERING MY NAME?!" The game perfectly captures the experience of being brave for exactly 17 seconds before a ghost starts humming in the hallway and your entire team turns into Olympic sprinters. Nothing builds friendship quite like abandoning your best friend in a dark basement because a thermometer said "slightly chilly." The ghosts are terrifying, the atmosphere is creepy, and the voice chat makes every panic attack sound cinematic. One moment you're carefully placing equipment; the next you're hiding in a closet listening to your friend scream, "IT'S RIGHT BEHIND ME!" followed by complete silence. 10/10. Would accidentally identify the ghost as the wrong species and get everyone haunted again. If you're looking for a game that combines genuine horror, detective work, and the emotional stability of a startled raccoon, Phasmophobia is a masterpiece. Just remember: the real ghost was the courage you lost along the way. 👻😂

This is one of the worst updates I've ever seen in a game. Before this, I'd have recommended it for sure. It wasn't pristine but at least for the most part it was responsive and ran on any device. Now there are dozens of new clunky animations that look horrible, lag and stutter and get you killed. Everyone around you in a lobby seems to stutter, the gear takes an age to actually appear in your hand in a usable way, not holding a camera facing down that you can't actually see through. The character creator is staggeringly bland. "Look at all our jewelry" but you can't change a characters hair. If this was playtested, which I doubt it was, this should never have been put out. Unless they revert this or redo it completely focusing on fixing the actual issues with the old build of the game, I won't be playing again.

I want to start by saying that I actually love this game, and I definitely recommend buying it, just not right now. The current state of the game is not as it was before the player character update, and new players would be severely underwhelmed by an otherwise great game. Until the major bugs are fixed, I simply can't encourage anyone to play this game. I hope this game gets its spark back, because what used to be the occasional, funny bug has become a glitchy nightmare. I am not spiteful or angry towards the dev team, I simply implore them to listen to their community and work as best they can towards making Phasmophobia fun again. I know it's hard, I know it's a lot of time and effort, and I know it's early access. However, this big update was a questionable move. I still adore this game and hope to see it flourish again.

I really, really hate writing a negative review about a game that I've loved for so long. I've poured hours and hours into Phasmophobia - it was a game that got me through some of the harder parts of my life. The atmosphere, the mechanics, the silly little bugs: I first played it and became obsessed. I've introduced it to so many of my friends! But in recent times: I've really found my love of the game waning. The Twitch drops were the first really intense sign of decline for me: they're always very limited, and there's no flexibility for timezones. I totally understand this isn't entirely in the team's control, but I'd definitely say pumping out limited time ID badges has made the game feel much more superficial. Patches have felt mostly aimed at punishing the community over fixing real issues. Obviously, the new patch hasn't helped things. I've only watched others play it through, but it's cemented for me that the atmosphere is just...gone. It honestly makes my heart hurt. HD maps and better player models only make a difference when the spirit of the game is maintained, and you reward the playerbase for engaging with the game directly. But it's sort of started to feel increasingly like the devs are going for style over substance, and brief touchpointing with special events over meaningful strategy development and nurturing the existing community. I'd love to edit this review and make it positive when these things are addressed. I've poured hundreds of hours into Phasmophobia, and I'm hopeful that it finds its way back to the janky, fun, lovable mess I always enjoyed. I'm ridiculously grateful for the scares and the friendships Phasmo has given me, and I hope there'll be more to come. But right now, I can't recommend the game in its current state. Please bring back my favourite game!

My friend and I got super into Phasmopobia. Unfortunately for us the game would break a month later. When you don't know if you messed up because of a legitimate player mistake or because of a game breaking bug what's the point in trying to get better? We had a hankering for a multiplayer, competitive game while not having to deal with team based play so Phasmopobia really appealed to us. We love horror and the basic concept of a game where you tread the line between dying and indentifying a ghost is really interesting. The technical aspect of getting better at recognising which ghost you are dealing with through repeated play (and of course wiki help) not only made the game replayable but also meant it would never get too easy. Unfortunately, despite any praise I might have for Phasmophobia, the game has numerous issues that result in the player experience being extremely unenjoyable. Trying to sound record the crucifix? Too bad, thats broken. Trying to loop the ghost? Well now you magically magnetise to every wall and object if you get too close. Trying to switch between items? Sometimes your character will hold their hand down for an extended period of time so you can't record that vital evidence. Trying to just HAVE an item? Sometimes in multiplayer they just completely disappear and only your friend can see them. My interest in improving at this game is quickly dwindling because despite Phasmophobia being very enjoyable the practically non-existent polish harms the game more than any lack of content updates would.

AMOGUS

Humanity spent centuries building cathedrals, landing on the Moon, and splitting the atom, only to arrive at the peak of entertainment: accusing your friends of being suspicious because they walked slightly too close to Electrical. Among Us is not merely a game. It is a masterpiece of psychological warfare, a social experiment, a test of friendship, and arguably the greatest achievement in human history. When I first launched Among Us, I expected a simple party game. What I received instead was a transformative experience that fundamentally changed the way I perceive reality. Every task is crafted with the precision of a master artist. Every emergency meeting feels like a courtroom drama. Every vent is a gateway to endless possibilities. The graphics are breathtaking. Some people claim the characters are just colorful beans. Those people fail to understand true artistic vision. The crewmates possess a level of visual perfection that rivals the finest works ever created. Their elegant movements, their iconic appearance, their tiny little legs carrying the fate of entire lobbies upon their shoulders. Absolute cinema. The gameplay is flawless. As a crewmate, you experience the thrill of desperately trying to complete tasks while wondering which of your trusted allies is secretly plotting your downfall. As an impostor, you become a master strategist capable of orchestrating schemes so intricate they would make world-class chess grandmasters question their life choices. The social deduction aspect is unparalleled. No other game has allowed me to witness someone commit a murder directly in front of five witnesses and still somehow convince the entire lobby that an innocent bystander was responsible. The level of persuasion, deception, and sheer confidence displayed in an average Among Us match should be studied by universities. The soundtrack deserves its own museum. Every sound effect carries emotional weight. The emergency meeting button alone generates more excitement than most blockbuster movies. Hearing that alarm instantly activates ancient survival instincts buried deep within the human brain. The replayability is infinite. I have played matches where nobody spoke. I have played matches where people delivered speeches longer than presidential addresses. I have seen friendships formed, friendships destroyed, and friendships rebuilt entirely because of events that occurred in a fictional spaceship populated by colorful beans. Among Us transcends gaming itself. It is philosophy. It is psychology. It is art. It is culture. It is the reason words like "sus" became part of everyday language. Few games can claim they altered global vocabulary. Among Us did it effortlessly. Every second spent in Among Us is a second spent experiencing peak entertainment. Whether you're solving tasks, calling emergency meetings, defending yourself against outrageous accusations, or watching your best friend somehow convince the lobby that oxygen is a government conspiracy, the game never fails to deliver unforgettable moments. 10/10. I entered expecting a game. I responded by yelling **"THE TEACHER IS SUS I SAW HER VENT"** and then **naruto running** around the room. Again, no one even **giggled.** I assume it's because it was forst bell and everyone was tired. My teacher said **"Please sit down."** But I wasn't about to give up. I made a last ditch effort to make everyone laugh. **I started to beatbox the Among Us theme song trap remix.** I was beatboxing it so well (i had practiced it at home), i was certain that everyone would burst out in laughter. As a casual player of this game, and some of the goofier premium vehicles it comes with, I do feel quite disheartened seeing the direction this game is going. No other experience captures the scale and complexity of the tech and vehicles provided in this game generally speaking, but the decision to continuously copy and paste vehicles and the continued implementation of overpriced and soulless premiums that also utilize AI for most of their included decals and graphics is a clear sign the developers at Gaijin have grown disenfranchised with their own creation and are simply using it for the cash-cow it is. Not to mention how many of the pre-existing issues that have been around for years now go unchecked. It's clear from the Nuclear Thunder event they have the capacity to make an engaging and quite enjoyable experience, but that was simply for April Fool's and while they are looking at bringing it back, they have already significantly degraded it compared to what it was before. While it could still be changed to reflect more of what it once was during that time frame, I have my doubts. They have no real incentive to change their current practices, and unless the community at large decides to refrain from purchasing the new DLC releases and strip them of the cashflow they provide, I do not imagine any improvements or existing issues will ever receive the attention they very clearly need. I'll still play, as will thousands of others, but it does frustrate me to see how much potential is being wasted for greed.

War Thunder. Oh Boy. This game is a emotional rollercoaster and one hell of a iceberg. On the Surface this game has a lot to offer. Planes, tanks, ships and even helicopters. The games developer is greedy and does not care about your needs, and only about money. this is demonstrated by the new Anime vehicle packs that are just copy paste from other ones, and lets be real, just gooner bait. You probably think that "Oh that's it? it does not seem too bad" well we have barley scratched the surface. I have spent 2000+ hours on this game and spent hundreds of dollars on a hope that my view of this game would improve and I would actually have fun. Because it is initially fun. but as you go deeper that fun turns into boredom, spite and the need to have the game be fun again. Spoiler, it does not. Especially because the last piece of fun, unique content was a limited time event that GAIJIN just f####d up in the end. If you want to explore this ♥♥♥♥♥♥ rabbit role further just go onto Youtube and search "the problem with war thunder". That should get you more insight. SUMMARY: If you’re a new player thinking about starting War Thunder, go in knowing what you’re signing up for. The early game is fun, but for many players that turns into a long grind built on frustration and longing for fun again. War Thunder is a strange game. It could be a great game, but it is not, due to corporate greed, laziness, and an inability to listen to the player base. For those who have never played the game, War Thunder is a semi-realistic military vehicle combat game that divides battles based on the capability, or Battle Rating (BR), of vehicles. BR often correlates with the period in which vehicles were developed, so players mostly battle each other in WWI-era (BR 1.0–4.0), WWII-era (BR 4.3–7.0), Cold War-era (BR 7.3–10.0), and modern battles (BR 10.3+) environments. War Thunder is a free-to-play, live-service game that monetizes specific content in the form of premium in-game currency (which is used to purchase vehicles, modifications) and premium time. Premium time speeds up research and the acquisition of the free in-game currency. It is particularly important for players who wish to progress through the research trees quickly, as research time increases significantly with the BR of the vehicles being researched. Even with premium time, researching top-tier vehicles often takes multiple weeks, if not months, assuming a skilled player with multiple hours of available playtime per day. It is clear that the system is aimed at hooking new players by feigning easy progression through low-tier research and then coercing them into buying premium content to ease and accelerate progression. Despite this, I would normally have said that War Thunder is a great game. A live-service game needs a source of income, and premium content here generally does not provide a competitive advantage. After all, I would rather progress more slowly than be forced to fight pay-to-win vehicles. However, my opinion of the game has changed over the past couple of years for several reasons: * The developers' inability to listen to the player base: The developers have consistently shown an unwillingness to engage with player feedback. Suggestions that would improve quality of life are ignored and replaced by unrequested features or additional premium content. Promised features are postponed indefinitely (e.g., improved bomber cockpits), and previous promises are broken (e.g., "We do not want new players buying their way into top tier," followed by the introduction of top-tier premium vehicles). * Laziness: Instead of adding vehicles unique to each nation that would fill gaps in their tech trees, vehicles are increasingly copy-pasted between nations. This is particularly noticeable at top tier, where almost any semblance of national distinctiveness has been erased. As of mid-2026, nearly every nation has a Leopard 2 or F-16 variant. This is especially frustrating because player suggestions on the forums are actively ignored, presumably to save development costs associated with creating new 3D assets. * Greed: Around 2025, prices for top-tier premium vehicles increased to €79.99 per vehicle. Yes, €79.99. You pay more for a single vehicle than for any new AAA releases. These vehicles are often not unique whatsoever and are frequently little more than copies of existing tech-tree vehicles. While I am generally opposed to locking original vehicles behind a paywall, Gaijin has demonstrated the ability to create interesting side-grades in the past, such as the TO-55, RDF/LT, and T29. * Use of AI: Gaijin has been caught multiple times employing AI-generated content, either directly or through third-party asset-generation companies. Profile pictures and decals have featured obvious AI-generated mistakes and even visible watermarks (e.g., Gemini). Instead of acknowledging the evidence, Gaijin has downplayed these incidents, claiming that AI is only used for testing, early-phase assets, and design concepts. One has to wonder how a price tag of €79.99 can be justified when corners are being cut at every opportunity and creativity is being replaced by automation, taking opportunities away from artists and content creators who design amazing vehicle skins and showcase them on the community forum. * Favoritism and bias: Gaijin Entertainment was originally founded in Russia. Around 2015, the company moved its headquarters to Hungary but retained a large Russian player base. Unfortunately, it is difficult to ignore the notion that the Russian tech tree receives preferential treatment (known in the community as russian bias). New features are introduced through Russian vehicles first (e.g., spall liners and DIRCM systems), and Russian top-tier lineups enjoy a competitive advantage in both ground and air battles. The interpretation of source material is fully selective. For russian equipment, photos and estimates often seem sufficient, whereas for NATO vehicles, even official documentation is misinterpreted, disregarded or discredited. NATO weapons such as the Spike and Brimstone missiles are underperforming, while Russian vehicles are overperforming: when Germany finally received a viable top-tier anti-aircraft system after years of waiting, Russia was given a helicopter equipped with DIRCM that effectively nullified it via a nonsensical magic forcefield. Instead of providing smaller nations with desperately needed filler vehicles, Russia. despite already having one of the strongest top-tier lineups, received the Pantsir-SM-SV under the justification that the existing system was underperforming, despite community-provided evidence that the supporting data was falsified. The final straw for many players was the addition of the BMPT and BMPT-72, vehicles that have come to fully dominate top-tier battles. These two vehicles received the typical "favorite child / money maker" treatment, with overperforming guns, ignored evidence regarding gun stabilization issues, and seemingly indestructible ammunition belts. Whether intentional or not, the perceived favoritism leaves battles feeling predictable, repetitive, and one-sided. And then, as sales of vehicles begin to stagnate, suddenly community-raised concerns are heard and nerfs are introduced (can't be hurting sales by nerfing too early!). So no, I can no longer recommend this game, and I will probably step away from it for the time being in the hope that more attention is drawn to these recent developments. This pattern of behavior has repeated itself for years, and meaningful change is long overdue.
